ground_truth.title: (Accidentally) Training a Dog → Cat Autoencoder
ground_truth.author: bittere
ground_truth.main_content: The author describes their journey of building an image autoencoder from scratch, only to discover that after 12+ hours of training, the model had inadvertently learned to transform dog features into cat-like features. This happened because the autoencoder was trained solely on cat images from a Kaggle dataset. The article explains how autoencoders work, their role in enabling diffusion models like Stable Diffusion to achieve 48x compression, and the technical challenges the author faced. The author presents various test results showing original images alongside their reconstructions, demonstrating how the model performs on images outside its training distribution - including dogs, text, and other subjects. The dog images particularly show interesting transformations where features like noses become more triangular (cat-like), while the model struggles with details like eyes and text. The author concludes that the autoencoder has learned both general-purpose image compression and feline-specific features, with these two learning objectives competing and causing interesting artifacts in non-cat images.
with: Imagine setting out to build something great; putting your blood, sweat and (computational) tears into building an image autoencoder, from scratch.
And after 12+ hours or arduous training, you realize you built a model that just replaces dog features with cat features.

The reason for this is most likely because the model was trained solely on cat images; while it did learn some general image compression, it also got good at compressing feline-specific features such as the nose, ears, etc.

Diffusion models are really resource-intensive for large images. Virtually all diffusion models available today, including Stable Diffusion and FLUX, are trained on a compressed latent space instead of normal pixel space.
The autoencoder was trained solely on cat images from this Kaggle dataset.
